region_of_origin: Sri Lanka / South Asia (widely cultivated globally)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 71765009 refers to cinnamon as a substance; no specific ground or powdered form code identified in SNOMED CT. Closest parent concept used.

main_ingredients: cinnamon bark (Cinnamomum verum or Cinnamomum cassia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground cinnamon is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.6 g), used as a spice in baking, beverages, or savoury dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Cinnamon, ground — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171320)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cinnamon, ground (FDC ID 171320) to confirm plausibility of all macro and micronutrient values. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spices — cinnamon, ground; analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Hlebowicz et al., 2007, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ground cinnamon is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database as a standalone food because it is consumed in very small quantities. A GI of approximately 5 is a widely cited analogous estimate for ground cinnamon based on its negligible available carbohydrate content after accounting for the very high dietary fibre (53.1 g/100g). Hlebowicz et al. (2007, AJCN) demonstrated that cinnamon significantly reduces postprandial blood glucose response. GL per serving = (GI x available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (5 x 2.1 g per 2.6 g serving) / 100 = 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (10 mg/100g), high potassium (431 mg/100g), and high dietary fibre (53.1 g/100g) with vasodilatory polyphenols
hypertension.guidance: Ground cinnamon is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; at typical culinary doses of 2–5 g per day, its sodium contribution is negligible (<0.5 mg), while its potassium content and cinnamaldehyde polyphenols support vascular tone — however, cinnamon-flavoured commercial products with added salt or sugar should be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Cinnamaldehyde and polyphenols improve insulin sensitivity; exceptionally high dietary fibre (53.1 g/100g) blunts postprandial glucose response; negligible net carbohydrate contribution at culinary doses
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground cinnamon is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; evidence from peer-reviewed studies (Hlebowicz et al., 2007, AJCN; Khan et al., 2003, Diabetes Care) supports its role in improving insulin sensitivity and reducing postprandial glucose — Cassia cinnamon should be limited to less than 1 teaspoon per day due to coumarin content, and Ceylon cinnamon is preferred for regular therapeutic use.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, minimal saturated fat (0.34 g/100g), high dietary fibre (53.1 g/100g), and anti-inflammatory cinnamaldehyde polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground cinnamon is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high fibre, and anti-inflammatory polyphenol profile support cardiovascular health at typical culinary doses, though high-dose Cassia cinnamon supplementation should be discussed with a clinician due to coumarin content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cinnamaldehyde and polyphenols inhibit NF-kB, reducing TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines relevant to RA; high calcium (1002 mg/100g) supports bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground cinnamon is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its anti-inflammatory polyphenols and high calcium content are beneficial, but Cassia cinnamon at high supplemental doses may interact with RA medications — Ceylon cinnamon is preferred for regular use.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground cinnamon is a finely milled, reddish-brown aromatic spice produced by drying and grinding the inner bark of Cinnamomum trees, primarily Cinnamomum verum (Ceylon cinnamon, considered true cinnamon) or Cinnamomum cassia (Cassia cinnamon, the more common commercial variety). It has a warm, sweet, slightly woody flavour with a characteristic aromatic intensity derived from its primary volatile compound, cinnamaldehyde. The texture is a fine, dry powder that disperses easily into both sweet and savoury preparations. Ground cinnamon is one of the world's oldest and most widely used spices, with origins in Sri Lanka and South Asia, and is now cultivated across tropical regions globally. It is used extensively in baking (cinnamon rolls, apple pie, biscuits), beverages (chai tea, mulled wine, coffee), breakfast foods (oatmeal, porridge), Middle Eastern and South Asian savoury dishes (biryanis, tagines, curries), and as a table condiment. Nutritionally, ground cinnamon is notable for its exceptionally high dietary fibre content (53.1 g/100g), very high calcium (1002 mg/100g), significant iron (8.32 mg/100g), and low sodium (10 mg/100g). Its carbohydrate content is 80.59 g/100g, but the majority is dietary fibre, making the net available carbohydrate very low at typical culinary serving sizes of 2–5 g. Clinically, cinnamaldehyde and polyphenolic compounds in cinnamon have demonstrated insulin-sensitising, anti-inflammatory, and vasodilatory properties in peer-reviewed research, making it a functionally relevant spice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Cassia cinnamon contains coumarin, which may be hepatotoxic at high daily doses; Ceylon cinnamon is preferred for regular therapeutic use.
common_user_queries: How much cinnamon should I use per day?
What is the difference between Ceylon and Cassia cinnamon?
Is ground cinnamon high in fibre?
Can I use cinnamon if I have high blood pressure?
What are the nutrients in ground cinnamon?
Is cinnamon safe for daily use?

ingredient_synonyms.cassia: Cinnamomum cassia, the common commercial cinnamon variety

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95, cross-referenced and confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cinnamon, ground (FDC ID 171320). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are well-documented in both approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate supported by peer-reviewed literature.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central; cinnamon is a plant-derived spice and naturally contains no cholesterol. 2. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100g — naturally zero as this is a minimally processed plant spice with no hydrogenated fats. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100g and Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100g — naturally zero in plant-derived spices; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. Saturated fat (0.34 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.09 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.07 g/100g), and all micronutrient values not present in the HPB input are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171320) for cinnamon, ground, which is an approved secondary source. 5. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'wholegrain' which is not applicable to a ground spice; this tag has been removed. 6. Cassia cinnamon contains coumarin at approximately 1–12 mg/g; the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) tolerable daily intake is 0.1 mg/kg body weight, corresponding to approximately 0.5–2 g Cassia cinnamon per day for a 60 kg adult. Ceylon cinnamon contains negligible coumarin. 7. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; ground cinnamon is not independently listed in the University of Sydney GI Database as a standalone food due to its use in very small culinary quantities. 8. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches based on publicly available ontology documentation. SNOMED CT 71765009 (Cinnamon substance) is used as closest_parent match — no specific ground or powdered cinnamon SNOMED CT code exists; the substance-level parent concept is the closest defensible anchor.
